questions.question: Why cannot a standard 2D CNN process a point cloud directly?
options: Point clouds are too large to fit in GPU memory

A CNN assumes input arranged on a regular spatial grid with fixed neighbourhood structure; a point cloud is an unordered set of points in R³ with no grid and variable size N, violating both assumptions simultaneously
CNNs only work on grayscale tensors, not 3D coordinate arrays
Point clouds require quaternion arithmetic that CNNs cannot perform
questions.explanation: CNN convolutions need a regular neighbourhood — the same kernel slides over every (i, j) position. A point cloud has no grid, no fixed neighbour relationship, and variable N. Voxelising recovers a grid (used by 3D CNNs) but is memory-expensive. PointNet side-stepped this with a permutation-invariant architecture that treats each point independently and then aggregates symmetrically.

questions.question: In the `PointNet` forward method, `torch.max(x, dim=-1)[0]` is applied after the two shared-MLP stages. What does this operation accomplish?
options: It selects the single most activated point to pass to the classifier, discarding all others
It applies batch normalisation across the point dimension to stabilise training

It is the symmetric aggregation step: max-pooling over the point dimension collapses all N per-point embeddings of shape (N, 1024) into a single global feature vector that is invariant to point ordering
It downsamples the point cloud from N points to a fixed 1024-point representation for the classifier head
questions.explanation: The symmetric-function trick is PointNet's core innovation. `torch.max` over the point axis picks the maximum activation across all N points independently per feature channel. Because max is commutative and associative, the result does not depend on point order — giving permutation invariance by construction. The resulting (batch, 1024) tensor feeds the MLP classifier head.

questions.question: The `positional_encoding` function maps a 3D point (D=3 coordinates) with L=10 frequency levels to how many output dimensions, and why?
options: 30 dimensions (3 coordinates × 10 frequencies, sine only)
10 dimensions (one output per frequency level, shared across coordinates)
20 dimensions (sine and cosine at 10 frequency levels for a single coordinate)
60 dimensions (each of the 3 coordinates produces 2×10 values — sine and cosine at each of the 10 frequencies 2⁰π through 2⁹π)
questions.explanation: For each scalar coordinate, positional encoding produces 2·L values: sin(2ˡπp) and cos(2ˡπp) for l=0…L-1. With L=10 that is 20 values per coordinate. A 3D point has 3 coordinates, so the total output is 3×2×10=60 dimensions. This lifts each low-frequency coordinate into a rich high-frequency feature space that the MLP can use to represent sharp geometry.

questions.question: In `volumetric_render`, the transmittance term `T_i = exp(-Σ_{j<i} σ_j·δ_j)` represents what physical quantity, and how does it affect the final pixel colour?
options: Transmittance — the fraction of light that survives without being absorbed by all samples before point i; samples behind opaque surfaces contribute near-zero colour because T_i ≈ 0 by the time the ray reaches them
The opacity of point i itself; higher σ at a sample means more light is absorbed there
The colour contribution of point i normalised by the total ray length
The expected depth of the nearest surface, used to weight nearer samples more heavily
questions.explanation: Transmittance T_i is the cumulative product of (1 - alpha) for all samples before i, encoding 'how much light survived to reach this point.' In an opaque region, sigma is large, alpha ≈ 1, and T_i for subsequent samples drops toward zero — exactly the physical behaviour of light blocked by dense matter. This is what makes NeRF's volume rendering physically principled and differentiable.

questions.question: How is a NeRF pixel colour computed during rendering, and why does this process never require explicit 3D ground-truth supervision?
options: As the output of the final MLP layer evaluated at the pixel's screen coordinates
By running a 2D convolution over a predicted depth map rendered from the implicit field
By looking up a precomputed voxel grid and trilinearly interpolating colour values

By casting a ray from the camera through the pixel, sampling N points along the ray, querying the MLP at each point for (density σ, colour c), and compositing with the volumetric rendering equation — comparing the rendered pixel against the actual photograph trains the MLP from 2D images alone
questions.explanation: NeRF rendering is classical volume rendering with a neural density field. For each pixel: pick a ray, sample along it, query (σ, c) at each sample, composite using (1 - exp(-σ·δ)) alphas and cumulative transmittance T_i. Backprop through this differentiable rendering step updates MLP weights to match training photographs. No depth sensors, no 3D meshes, no explicit geometry supervision ever appear.

questions.question: Why has 3D Gaussian splatting largely replaced vanilla NeRF in production pipelines despite NeRF still being the conceptual foundation?
options: 3D Gaussian splatting uses an explicit representation — millions of 3D Gaussians with learnable position, opacity, colour, and covariance — that renders in real time via GPU rasterisation instead of per-pixel MLP queries; training takes minutes rather than hours, and rendering is 100× faster at comparable reconstruction quality
Gaussian splatting produces strictly higher-quality images because Gaussians are a better prior for real-world geometry
NeRF was mathematically shown to overfit to training views in ways Gaussian splatting avoids by construction
Gaussians are more compressible for streaming applications, reducing bandwidth by 10× over NeRF checkpoints
questions.explanation: 3D Gaussian Splatting (SIGGRAPH 2023) replaces the implicit MLP scene with an explicit cloud of 3D Gaussian primitives rendered by GPU rasterisation — orders of magnitude faster than per-pixel ray sampling through an MLP. Most 2026 NeRF products ship with Gaussian splatting or its successors; the NeRF volumetric-rendering paradigm still informs the training objective and the math.
